content.problemStatement:
Operator overloading in C++ allows you to define how operators should behave when used with user-defined data types (classes and structures). \
It allows you to customize the behavior of operators to work with objects of your own classes. 

In the `operator` function, you specify how the operator should work with objects of your class.\
This function should be a member function of the class and should have a special name and return type based on the operator you're overloading.

This code is an example for operator overloading where '+' operator is overloaded as follows -
- We define a Complex class to represent complex numbers. A complex number consists of two parts: real and imaginary. The Complex class has a constructor that takes two integers (_real and _imaginary) as parameters and initializes the real and imaginary member variables with the provided values.
- The code overloads the + operator for the Complex class. The + operator is defined as a member function inside the class with the signature Complex operator+(const Complex& other). This operator function takes another Complex object (other) as a constant reference.
- Inside the operator+ function, it calculates the sum of the real parts and the sum of the imaginary parts separately. It then creates a new Complex object with these calculated values and returns it. This allows us to add two Complex objects together using the + operator as if they were mathematical complex numbers.
- In the main function, two Complex objects (c1 and c2) are created, each initialized with specific real and imaginary parts. c1 represents the complex number 1 + 2i, and c2 represents 3 + 4i. The overloaded + operator is used to add c1 and c2 together, resulting in a new Complex object called result.

### Task
**Run the code to check the output.**
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
